PO. BOX 80"7 • SACR/.-MfNTO. 95804 •.. 
00 K STREET BUILDING. SACRAMfNl0. 9"ii1 
Edward F. Smith, Attorney 
Governmental Affairs Div sion 
rhe Du Pont Company 
Legal Department, Room 7029 
Du Pont Building 
Wilmington, Delaware 
19898 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
August 1 
i 
1977 
Re: 
Opinion Request 
No. 77 -014 
Thank you for your letter 0f May 10, 1977, in 
which you request an opinion with respect to 
~he repo-ting 
provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
Since your letter 
does not raise a substantial question under th 
Act, no 
formal opinion will be issued. 
'lowever t 
I hope the 
followi::(~ 
informal comments will be helpful. 
You ask whether the Du Font Company can underwrit p 
the expenses involved in flying a member of an organic solv~nt3 
subcommittee of the California Air Resources Board from 
San Diego, California, to a Du Pont Company installation in 
Troy, Michigan. 
The purpose of the trip is to give the 
subcommittee member a first-hand view of the operations of a 
"refinish spray booth." 
The organic solvents subcommittee 
is an advisory task force established to assist the ARB 
staff in the drafting of regulations. 
The member in questj~n 
sits as an unsalaried member of the advisory subcommittee 
and is a full-time employee of the San Diego County Air 
Polution Control Board. 
Although he occasionally receives 
travel expenses from the state, he is not a state employee. 
Specifically, you ask whether the Political Reform 
Act prohibits Du Pont from providing free transportation and 
accommodations to the subcommittee member for the purpose of 
permitting him to view the spray booth in Michigan. 
As we 
explain more fully below, the Act does not prohibit Du Pont 
from underwriting these travel expenses. 
The Act does re-
quire, however, that the payments be reported properly by 
Du Pont on its Lobbyist Employer Statement (Form 650) and by 


Edward F. Sm th, Attorney 
August 19, 
9 7 
Page Two 
the subcommittee official on his Statement of Economic In-
terests. 
Furthermore, if the trip is considered a gift to 
the official, the official may be required to disqualify 
himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego 
County Air Pollution Control Board concerning Du Pont. 
No 
disqualification would be required, however, in connection 
with the officialfs duties on the advisory subcommittee to 
the State Air Resources Board. 
I will develop each of these 
points. 
The possibility that payment for the trip by Du Pont 
mightl~e prohibited arises from Government Code Section 
86203-
of the Act which bars a lobbyist from making, arranging, 
or acting as an agent or intermediary in the making of a 
gift of $10 or more in a calendar month to a state "agency 
official." 
This prohibition, however, does not apply to the 
instant case. 
An unsalaried member of an advisory board is 
not an "agency official ft within the meaning of the Act (see 
opinion requested by John C. Morrissey, 2 FPPC Ops. 84 (No. 
75-099, July 6, 1976)). 
The Air Resources Board advises us 
that the organic solvents subcommittee is strictly an advisorv 
committee with no decision making functions. 
Therefore, no 
prohibitions apply, and not only is it permissible for Du Pont 
to pay for the trip, but it would also be permissible for a 
lobbyist to be involved in arranging the trip. 
It is required, however, that the payments involved 
be properly reported by both Du Pont and the official. 
Since it is the employer of a lobbyist, Du Pont must file a 
Form 650 disclosing all "payments to influence legislative 
or administrative action" (Section 86109). 
We regard the 
payments for this trip as "payments .•. in connection with 
direct communication with any ... agency official" within 
the meaning of Section 82045(d), and they should therefore 
be reported on part 2, schedule i of Du Pont's Form 650. 
We 
reach this conclusion because, although the subcommittee 
member is not himself an "agency official," he can be expecte,j 
to communicate with agency officials of the Air Resources 
Board as a result of his trip. 
Thus, "direct communication" 
within the meaning of Section 82045(d) would occur here. 
With respect to the subcommittee member, he may 
have to disclose receipt of the trip as a gift on the Statement 
of Economic Interests he files in connection with his employment 
II All statutory references are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise noted. 


Edward F. Smith, Attorney 
August 19, 1977 
Page Three 
on the San Diego Air Pollution Control Board. 2 
Moreover, 
regardless 
f whether the official has a disclosure obligation, 
if the trip is a gift to him, he may be required to disqualify 
himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego 
Air Pollution Control Board respecting Du Pont. 
Therefore, two questions are raised. 
First, what 
portion of a trip such as this is a "gift"? 
Second, to the 
extent the trip is a gift, would it be a g~7t to the official 
or would it be a gift to San Diego County,-
thereby relieving 
the official from any disclosure or disqualification obligati ns? 
with respect to the first question, the cost of 
the transportation from San Diego to Troy and payments for 
the official's food, accommodation and entertainment during 
his stay in Troy would be a gift. 
Any costs incurred in 
connection with the official's tour and inspection of Du Pont's 
facilities, however, would not be a gift since the Commission 
has concluded that such costs are in the nature of "informa-
tional material." 
"Informational material" is specifically 
excluded from the Act's definition of "gift." 
(Section 
82028; see opinion requested by John Spellman, 1 FPPC ops. 
16 (No. 75-026, May 1, 1975).) 
To the extent a gift is involved, the next 
question is whether the gift is to the official or to San Diego 
County. 
The Commission has held in a recent opinion that if 
certain criteria are met, a benefit received by an official 
in connection with his official duties will be considered a 
gift to his government employer. 
In such a case, the official 
does not have to disclose receipt of the gift on his Statement 
of Economic Interests and need not disqualify himself from 
£/ 
The subcommittee member has no disclosure 
obligations with respect to his service on the Air Resources 
Board since he serves in a purely advisory capacity. 
However, 
he may have disclosure obligations in connection with his 
San Diego County employment if the San Digeo County Air 
Pollution Control Board now has a conflict of interest code 
in effect and if the code requires the official to disclose 
gifts of this type. 
l/ 
We are advised by the Air Resources Board that 
if the official sought reimbursement for this trip, he would 
have to apply to his employer, the San Diego Air Pollution 
Control Board. 
He would not be eligible for reimbursement 
from the state. 
Therefore, if any government entity receives 
a gift as a result of this transaction, it would be the 
County of San Diego. 


Edward F. Smith, Attorney 
August 19, 
977 
e Four 
deci ions concerning the donor of the 9 
requested 
Peter G. Stone, 3 FPPC Ops. 
003, June 
1977), a 
copy~f which I 
s 
e opin on 
(~o. 77-
Applying the standards set fort 
the "Stone 
opinion to your case, the official would 
have to report 
receipt of the trip and 
~ould not have 
di gu lify ~imsel~ 
from deci~;ions concernincJ Du Pont if D.: Pont 
akes tl:e trip 
available for use by San Diego County without specifyinq 
whom the recipient should be, leaving that decision to the 
County. 
Since out-of-state travel by employees of San Dieg 
County must be approved by the County Board of Supervisors, 
the trip must be described in and accepted by a public re-
solution adopted by that body. 
If those standards are met, 
the trip would be considered a gift to the government entit 
rather than the official. 
Even if the standards are not met, however, and 
the official is considered to have received a gift, he woul 
only have possible disqualification obligation 
with r speer 
to the decisiog, he makes on the San Diego Air Pollution 
Control Board.-
He would not be required to disqualify 
himself from participation in any activities of the organic 
solvents subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board since 
he serves in only an advisory capacity on that Board. 
I trust the foregoing answers your question. 
If 
you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you 
may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. 
I 
4/ 
This discussion assumes that the official is 
not provided with goods or services over and above those 
ordinarily necessary for a trip of this nature. 
If he is, 
these benefits would be gifts to the official even if his 
plane fare, food and lodging are not because they are con-
sidered to be gifts to his employer. 
5/ 
The official would be required to disqualify 
himself if he receives gifts worth $250 or more from Du Pont 
during the 12 months preceding the decision in question and 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on Du Pont, distinguishable from 
the effect on the public generally. 
Sections 87100 and 
87103. 


Edward F. Smith, Attorney 
August 19,177 
Page Five 
apologize 
r the delay in responding to your letter but 
appreciate the cooperation you have given our staff in de-
ve oping the factual background of your inquiry_ 
If we can 
be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to call me 
or Ted Prim, an attorney in the Commission's Legal Division. 
MB:MJB:plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
J

Mich~ B~nnett 
Executive Director

Thank you very much, .in ant icipation of your prom:d~

sections.conclusion: The Act does not prohibit Du Pont from underwriting these travel expenses. However, the Act requires that the payments be reported properly by Du Pont on its Lobbyist Employer Statement (Form 650) and by the subcommittee official on his Statement of Economic Interests. Furthermore, if the trip is considered a gift to the official, the official may be required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board concerning Du Pont. No disqualification would be required in connection with the official's duties on the advisory subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Du Pont Company legally pay for travel expenses to transport a California Air Resources Board advisory subcommittee member to view Du Pont facilities in Michigan under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Du Pont may pay for the trip because the subcommittee member is not an "agency official" under the Act, but the payments must be properly reported on Du Pont's Form 650 and the official's Statement of Economic Interests. The official may face disqualification requirements for San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board decisions if the trip constitutes a gift, though specific criteria can classify it as a gift to the government entity rather than the individual.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated May 10, 1977 referenced, with apparent duplicate May 10 letter shown) and the FPPC response (dated August 19, 1977). FPPC states 'no formal opinion will be issued' but provides detailed informal comments. OCR quality is moderate with some character recognition issues but extractable. Multiple footnotes provide important clarifications on disqualification requirements and gift characterization.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit Du Pont from underwriting these travel expenses. However, the Act requires that the payments be reported properly by Du Pont on its Lobbyist Employer Statement (Form 650) and by the subcommittee official on his Statement of Economic Interests. Furthermore, if the trip is considered a gift to the official, the official may be required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions made by the San Diego County Air Pollution Control Board concerning Du Pont. No disqualification would be required in connection with the official's duties on the advisory subcommittee to the State Air Resources Board.

embedding.summary: FPPC informal opinion denying a formal opinion but providing guidance that Du Pont Company may legally underwrite travel expenses for a California Air Resources Board advisory subcommittee member to visit Du Pont facilities in Michigan, subject to proper reporting and potential gift/disqualification considerations.
